author: @VeniceInventors
text: Some people fear what they don't understand, and if they see stories about the dangers of AI, it only magnifies their fears. Is AI dangerous? No. Can AI be dangerous? Yes. You can replace "AI" with "pillow" and the same answers still apply. It all depends on how it is used. As investors got disillusioned with quantum computing, AI became the next hyped news item, but it's been around for decades. Its usefulness is most obvious when used to sift through massive amounts of data to narrow it down to a small amount of relevant data. It's completely useless to come up with new data as it can only operate on existing data, so no, it won't find a way to get rid of those annoying organic meat bags. But it can be used to very effectively figure out the best traffic lights timing to keep traffic moving and reduce pollution, if only someone would use it for that.

author: @Alkimi
text:
What we do is take readings from one of the radios on the device, or from the microphone, to get a real-time sample of noise, and then use that to get proper randomness.
It's arguably just as random as humans are, since humans are also affected by their surroundings and their past influences.

I believe creation requires free-will, or inspiration, the ability to muse, wax philosophical, ponder, dream, imagine, divine, yearn, or tire.
No amount of random sample data and algorithms can invoke the Deus Ex Machina, in my opinion.

author: @Friedfish-zm7fx
text:
Robert Murray has a keener insight into AI than most pundits.

My take on AI and randomness.

The following is a comment I gave at a meeting about Artificial Intelligence (AI) (among other topics).

I applaud one of the panelist who brought up the subject of the Turing Test. The AI may pass off as a human being but said "human" may be a sociopath. The current stage of AI is really one of Artificial Cleverness. I see AI as a technology capable of seeking and acquiring new knowledge, not some technology that is a search engine on steroids and a parser of human languages.

Besides Alan Turing, may I bring up 2 other mathematicians: Godel and Church. From Godel one knows that Mathematics is never-ending, to solve some problems new axioms need to be invoked. Church addressed the question: can a machine with associated hardware, software, algorithms can derive all possible mathematical facts? Answer is: No. At first glance it seems that humans are needed forever in the quest of intelligence and knowledge. But is that so?

Randomness. From Randomness arose Life. The subcomponents of Life (DNA, RNA, etc..) are akin to computer programs: a result of, a manifestation of Intelligence. Said Intelligence (and its products) are derived from Randomness and so far one does not have a clue how. From the Second Law of Thermodynamics a system can go from random to ordered state with the consequence that the surroundings become more random. So randomness can become less random or more random. One is utterly clueless about the dynamics of randomness. Yes, I know all about Chaos Theory.

Randomness. From Randomness arose Humans. Through the Ages, Humans acquired Knowledge. How? No extra-terrestials came to Earth and passed down Knowledge to the mere mortal humans. Where did Knowledge come from? Answer: Randomness of the Universe. Somehow, the Human Brain interacts with randomness (via quantum or non-quantum ways) and some how (one is still clueless) extracts Logic, Knowledge, and Meaning. If ever one builds a computer chip that can extract Logic, Knowledge, and Meaning from surrounding Randomness, the gig is up for the Human Race.

Randomness. From Randomness comes Matter (ordered, physical). From Randomness comes Knowledge (ordered, metaphysical).

Randomness. The Ancient Greek philosopher Epicurius was the first to hypothesis the atomic (indivisible matter) theory of matter. The genius of Epicurius is that he hypothesized that the atom-atom interactions are random (inspired by Brownian motion of pollen in water?) and such randomness brings about free will. The question of free will may be answered when after building the computer mentioned in the previous paragraph and replies "NO!!!" when givien a command by a human.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Thanks for the video. AI is really making huge strides. Just a few days ago I was watching Sora (full color video clips no sound), and today I am watching EMO (Full color video of people talking based on a single source image, and an audio recording). You can imagine if these 2 technologies were combined... it would literally transform the entertainment industry overnight... and no doubt in less than a few years, it will be transformed. 
   As for Solid State batteries... the ability of AI to come up with new solutions quickly -- well that's what we have all been hoping for !  The real question is going to be how do we adapt safely to all of this. World economies will have to change in new ways.
   And you are correct, that AI will be not have the same "type" of creativity, the AI may not see new ways to do things, but simply iterate over the possibilities that it has access to. Our job as inventors will be to use the AI tools to see beyond the limits of our imagination.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: AI is a tool, at this point it would appear to be analogous to nuclear energy.  Regardless of how much good it does it will only take 1 or 2 atomic bomb equivalents for it to be a net detriment given how interconnected the worlds computer systems are.  There is already a growing gap between the have's and have nots, what is to prevent the wealthy from using AI to increase that disparity.  If it can do 20 years work in 80 hours how safe are our personal accounts, such as banking, from this?  The problem with technologies like these is few if any people understand how it works and it is released without adequate safeguards 1st being developed.  AI certainly has incredible potential, unfortunately that is a 2 edged sword and mighty sharp.

author: @dgpreston5593
text: Rob, we may have divergent perspectives here regarding randomness as the core tenet of creativity.  You make a great case for serendipity, but that's recognition of an unplanned result. What we recently started calling A.I. is more akin to our prefrontal cortex, enabling search of a large state space that's been trained by exposure to observations.  In my view, intuition guides investigation wrt these observations. I agree it is a tool, and works much differently than people commonly think.  Human creativity, in general, is based upon informed selection among known or random options.  AI "creativity" is based upon a maximized, ranked utility score among all combinations of configuration of the vast training space of all training examples.

author: @DerekRoss1958
text:
I agree that thetes a connection between randomness and creativity. However the problem with computers and creativity is actually too much randomness rather than too little. It's quite easy to write a little program that will generate any novel whatsoever using a random number generator. In fact you can even do it with Scrabble tiles and a hat to pick them out of. The real problem is that the program is too random and what it writes isn't even English, never mind a story. The computer is being too "creative".

What AI does is to reduce the "creativity" to the point where something sensible, and perhaps useful, is created. The trick is to reduce the randomness that far, but not so far that all the creativity disappears.

author: @PaulBrunt
text: I think you might be attributing too much to randomness. Generative AI relies on randomness to generate content, but the key to creativity isn't just randomness; it's the ability to recognize when a random event falls within a search domain. Essentially, what humans do—and what AI currently struggles with—is truly recognizing what the search space encompasses. For example, a random idea might be classified by an AI as outside the search space, but a human would recognize it's still within it. How long humans will maintain this advantage is a matter for debate.

author: @npsit1
text: 4:19 I seem to remember many years ago, there was a video here on Youtube that discussed a passenger jet manufacturer which used an early AI system to create a lighter and stronger bulkhead. I wish I could find it. Basically they told it was they wanted it to do and specified the parameters for dimensions and let it go. It designed a very effective bulkhead that was both lighter and used less material but was stronger than traditional welding or riveting. Companies had also used computer systems for testing antenna designs. It ran through hundreds or even thousands of permutations before selecting a design based on the given parameters. I feel like an AI system could be used to do something similar, but maybe more quickly.

author: @EarnieBoyd
text: Be careful with AI, it isn't always correct. For instance today I asked ChatGPT 4 to give me the answer to 3/16 - 9/20 and it spit back an answer of 11/80. Then I asked it to explain the answer and it corrected itself by stepping through the problem.

author: @CCoburn3
text: Years ago, they used mules for plowing. My grandfathers both plowed with mules. But then the tractor came along, and people who learned how to use a tractor could do more work in a day than several people who used mules. My grandfathers learned how to use tractors. But there were people who never learned. And these people eventually lost out. But those who learned to use the new tool (i.e., the tractor) prospered. The same thing is going on with AI. AI will allow a skillful person to be much more productive. And that is going to result in people who do not develop skill in using AI to lose out. But those who learn how to best use this tool will prosper.

replies.author: @ablacknambercat
replies.text: AI is a tool and like all tools, it will let people do stuff that used to require specialist skills. Or put another way, now far more people can do stuff and the benefits are far more available and affordable. This is a good thing.

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text: @ablacknambercat Sure. But we shouldn’t forget that specialists will be able to do amazing things that are beyond the ability of the average person. I can use a table saw to build bookshelves. Before the table saw, that sort of thing would have taken more time and skill than I possess. But there are people who have studied woodworking who can use the table saw to make things I will never be able to make. The same thing applies to AI. There will be many journeyman AI users who will be able to be more productive. But there will also be masters who will know how to get the most out of AI. These people will be in high demand.
